title: Joint adoptions by same-sex partners
subtitle: Same-sex partners can adopt a child legally, with both parents being recognized as such.
caption:
Joint adoptions by same-sex partners

Same-sex partners can adopt a child legally, with both parents being recognized as such.

This choropleth world map displays the legal status of joint adoptions by same-sex partners as of 2025. The legend categorizes countries into eight distinct statuses: Legal, Married couples only, Second parent adoption only, Varies by region, Individual only, Ambiguous, Illegal, and No data. The blue color, representing "Legal" status, covers the United States, Canada, most of Western Europe, Australia, New Zealand, and several countries in South America including Argentina and Brazil. Red areas, indicating "Illegal" status, are prominent across much of Africa, the Middle East, Russia, and parts of Asia. Yellow shades denote "Individual only" adoption rights and appear in countries such as Mexico, India, and parts of Eastern Europe. Green areas, signifying "Married couples only," are visible in a few European nations. Orange regions labeled "Ambiguous" include countries like Colombia and Thailand. Grey hatching indicates "No data" and is present in parts of Central Africa and the Middle East. The map shows a concentration of legal recognition in the Americas and Western Europe, while large portions of Africa and Asia fall into the illegal category. Some countries show regional variation or limited recognition through individual or second-parent adoption pathways. The visual distribution highlights significant global disparities in the legal framework surrounding same-sex joint adoption rights.
